title: Labneh
subtitle: Strained yoghurt cheese with olive oil, dried mint, and salt.
excerpt: Yoghurt strained through muslin overnight until it firms into a soft, tangy cheese. Spread on a plate, anointed with olive oil and dried mint, eaten with warm flatbread for breakfast or as part of a mezze spread. Five minutes of work and one night of patience.
sourceNotes:
Levantine and eastern Mediterranean home cookery, particularly Lebanese, Syrian, and Palestinian kitchens where labneh is a daily breakfast and mezze staple. Documented in Claudia Roden's Book of Middle Eastern Food and in twentieth-century Arabic cookbooks.

The technique predates written recipes. Yoghurt strained of whey was a way of extending dairy in warm climates before refrigeration. Modern versions sit somewhere between fresh cheese and thick yoghurt; the longer the drain, the firmer the labneh.

Kept in olive oil, labneh balls last weeks in the fridge and turn into a small pantry resource for breakfasts and lunches.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. Use a larger sieve or two smaller ones; the yoghurt needs room for the whey to drain.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Start the day before you want to eat it. Twelve to twenty-four hours of straining is the right window; longer produces a firmer cheese.

recipeTools.notes: Or a clean tea towel. The cloth has to be fine enough to hold the curds without letting them through.
Goes under the sieve to catch the whey.

glossaryTerms.definition: The watery yellowish liquid that separates from yoghurt as it strains. Strained off and discarded for plain labneh; tipped back to thin the cheese later if needed. Some bakers keep the whey for bread dough; it is a mild leaven.

content.text: Labneh is yoghurt strained until it firms up into a soft, tangy cheese, somewhere between Greek yoghurt and ricotta. Spread on a plate, drizzled with olive oil, sprinkled with dried mint and a little salt, mopped up with warm flatbread. The breakfast that holds up Levantine kitchens; the cold mezze that arrives next to hummus.
Five minutes of work. The rest is the cloth, the sieve, and the night. Twelve to twenty-four hours of straining lets the
whey
separate from the curds; the cheese that remains is two-thirds the volume of the yoghurt you started with.
The only piece of kit that matters is a fine cloth. Muslin is cleanest; a thin tea towel works. Cheesecloth is fine if you double it up.
What you need

items.prepNote: full-fat Greek-style; do not use low-fat
dried, crumbled — or 1 tbsp fresh chopped

content.text: Method
Salt the yoghurt

Tip {{greek-yoghurt}} of Greek yoghurt into a bowl. Add {{sea-salt-fine}} of fine salt and stir through. The salt draws water out of the yoghurt and speeds the strain by a couple of hours; it also seasons the cheese as it forms.
Set up the strain

Line a sieve with a double layer of muslin or a clean thin tea towel. Set the sieve over a deep bowl, leaving at least 5 cm of clearance between the sieve base and the bottom of the bowl. Scrape the yoghurt into the lined sieve, gather the corners of the cloth, and twist gently to form a bag.

Tie the gathered cloth with kitchen string, leaving a long tail. Suspend the bag over the bowl by hooking the string over a wooden spoon laid across the bowl, so the cheese hangs in the sieve without touching the bottom. The whey drips into the bowl below.
Strain in the fridge overnight

Slide the bowl and sieve into the fridge for twelve hours minimum, eighteen to twenty-four for a firmer cheese. Tip the whey out of the bowl halfway through if it climbs close to the sieve; the cheese should sit out of any liquid.

After twelve hours, the labneh holds a soft cream-cheese shape. After twenty-four, it is firm enough to hold a clean spoon-line. After thirty-six, firm enough to roll into balls for storing in oil. Choose the texture that suits the meal.
Plate and dress

Scrape the labneh out of the cloth onto a wide flat plate. Spread to a shallow disc with the back of a spoon, leaving the surface a little rough. Drizzle {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil across the top in a slow spiral. Crumble {{mint}} of dried mint between your fingers and scatter over. Add lemon zest if using.
Serve with warm pitta, flatbread, or sourdough. A handful of olives and a sliced tomato turn it into breakfast; a longer mezze spread sits the labneh next to hummus and mutabal.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Labneh is forgiving. The two failure modes are watery cheese and sour cheese.

items.symptom: Labneh still wet after twelve hours.
items.cause: Yoghurt was thin, or strain did not have room.
items.fix: Some yoghurts (particularly cheaper supermarket brands) are stabilised and drain slowly. Press the cloth gently to squeeze out more whey, then return to the fridge for a few more hours. Thin yoghurt may need 24 to 30 hours.

items.cause: Yoghurt was too tangy to begin with, or strained too long at room temperature.
items.fix: Use a good-quality full-fat Greek yoghurt; cheaper yoghurts can be acidic. Always strain in the fridge, never on the counter; the cheese sours fast at warmer temperatures.

items.cause: Cloth too coarse or a tear.
items.fix: Double up the cloth if using a thin tea towel; muslin can be doubled too. Check for holes before lining the sieve.

items.symptom: Yoghurt clumped on the cloth and the centre stayed wet.
items.cause: The bag did not have room for the whey to escape.
items.fix: Untie the bag, fold the labneh back on itself once, and re-tie. Suspended bags drain more evenly than yoghurt sitting flat in a sieve.

content.text: Variations

Strain for thirty-six to forty-eight hours and the cheese will be firm enough to roll into walnut-sized balls between oiled palms. Drop the balls into a sterilised jar of olive oil with a sprig of thyme and a few peppercorns; refrigerated, they keep for two to three weeks and turn into a small store-cupboard resource.

Sweeten the cheese with a spoon of honey, a sprinkle of crushed pistachios, and a drizzle of pomegranate syrup for an unconventional dessert that sits between cheesecake and yoghurt. Or fold chopped fresh mint and grated garlic through for a dip closer to a Greek tzatziki.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers

Plain labneh keeps four to five days in a covered container in the fridge. Stir before using if surface moisture has risen. Labneh balls in oil keep two to three weeks in a sealed jar in the fridge; pour off the oil to top up the salads they go into.
Labneh does not freeze; the texture breaks when defrosted. Make smaller batches rather than freezing.
Where this dish lives

Labneh is one of the older dairy preparations of the eastern Mediterranean, recorded in Arabic and Levantine cookery long before refrigeration. The technique of straining yoghurt extended a perishable into a soft cheese that could be kept for days or, with the oil-and-jar treatment, for weeks. In Lebanon, Syria, Palestine, and Jordan, labneh is a daily breakfast; in Greek and Turkish kitchens, very similar cheeses appear under different names.

The dressing varies. Lebanese kitchens favour olive oil and dried mint; Syrian ones add za'atar; Palestinian breakfasts often add a sliced fresh tomato and a clutch of olives. Each version is a small statement of place. The strained cheese underneath is the same.
